title: Keypoint-Based Detection (CornerNet)
concept: CornerNet detects objects by predicting heatmaps for top-left and bottom-right corners and pairing them via embeddings.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main issue with using rigid anchor box templates?

options.text: They struggle to fit highly variable or irregular object shapes
They only support black and white images
They prevent the use of convolutional layers

exercises.explain: Correct! Rigid templates struggle with irregular shapes and require dataset-specific tuning.

cards.title: Corner Marks
cards.body: What if, instead of guessing a box size, we just locate the boundaries of an object by marking its corners? In 2018, a model called CornerNet did exactly this, pioneering keypoint-based detection. Let's see how it works.

cards.title: Keypoint-Based Detection (CornerNet)
cards.body: CornerNet is an anchor-free detector that represents an object as a pair of keypoints: a top-left corner and a bottom-right corner. The network outputs heatmaps for these corners and groups them using associative embedding (matching tags).

cards.title: Framing a Painting
cards.body: Imagine placing a frame around a painting. You don't try to guess box sizes first. You just mark the top-left corner of the canvas and the bottom-right corner with a pencil, then connect them. That is CornerNet!

cards.title: Corner Heatmaps
cards.body: For a cat, CornerNet predicts a heatmap peaking at the cat's top-left corner and another at its bottom-right corner. It uses matching ID numbers (embeddings) predicted at each corner to know these two corners belong to the same cat.

cards.title: Are Corners Easy?
cards.body: You might think corners are easy to find because they are distinct. Fact: The corner of an object's bounding box is often empty background space outside the actual object (like the corner of a round ball), making it tricky to predict.

left.text:
Detects top-left & bottom-right points
Predicts coordinates independently
No anchor templates used

right.text:
Generates matching tags for corners
Groups corners with similar tags
Draws final bounding box

exercises.prompt: How does CornerNet define the location of an object?

options.text: By predicting its top-left and bottom-right corners
By calculating the center point and the radius
By drawing a polygon mask around the pixels
By dividing the object into 10 slices

exercises.explain: Correct! CornerNet represents objects using their top-left and bottom-right corners.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Associative embedding is a technique used to match corresponding top-left and bottom-right corners belonging to the same object.
exercises.explain: True. The model predicts a tag value for each corner; corners with similar tag values are grouped together.

exercises.sentence: CornerNet is a keypoint-based detector that eliminates the need for ___ boxes.
exercises.explain: CornerNet is anchor-free, locating corners directly.

exercises.prompt: What is a main challenge when predicting box corners?

options.text: The corner of a bounding box is often empty background space containing no object features
Corners have too many overlapping colors
The coordinates must be positive numbers

exercises.explain: Correct! Bounding box corners often lie in the background, making it hard for the network to extract local object features.

exercises.prompt: You are detecting multiple overlapping people in a photo. CornerNet detects three top-left corners and three bottom-right corners. How does it know which top-left corner matches which bottom-right corner?

options.text: It compares the associative embedding tags predicted for each corner, grouping corners that have the closest tag values.
It always matches the top-left corner to the nearest bottom-right corner.
It uses a classification model to sort them.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Associative embedding tags act as unique identifiers to link matching keypoints together.
